1: הַשָּׁמַיִם מְסַפְּרִים כְּבוֹד-אֵל (hashāmayim mĕsappĕrîm kĕbôd-ʾêl, "The heavens declare the glory of God") — the participle mĕsappĕrîm (from sāpar: to count, recount, narrate): an ongoing, unceasing declaration. The heavens are not said to have declared or to eventually declare — they are perpetually in the act of narrating. וּמַעֲשֵׂה יָדָיו מַגִּיד הָרָקִיעַ (ûmaʿăśê yādāyw maggîd hārāqîaʿ, "the expanse proclaims his handiwork") — rāqîaʿ: the firmament/expanse (from rāqaʿ, to hammer/beat out thin). The beaten-out sky continually announces (participle) the work of God's hands.
2: יוֹם לְיוֹם יַבִּיעַ אֹמֶר (yôm lĕyôm yabbiʿa ʾōmer, "Day after day pours out its speech") — nābaʿ (hiphil: to pour out, to bubble up): the image of water welling up from a spring — speech as inexhaustible, effervescent outpouring. ʾŌmer: speech, word (a poetic word). וְלַיְלָה לְּלַיְלָה יְחַוֶּה-דָּעַת (wĕlaylāh llĕlaylāh yĕḥawweh-dāʿat, "and night after night reveals knowledge") — ḥāwāh (piel: to show, to tell, to reveal): night declaring what day cannot. The entire calendar — every day, every night — participates in the continuous revelation.
3: אֵין-אֹמֶר וְאֵין דְּבָרִים (ʾên-ʾōmer wĕʾên dĕbārîm, "There is no speech, there are no words") — the paradox that defines the whole passage: the declaration of vv1-2 is made in no human language. Bĕlî nishmaʿ qôlām: "without their voice being heard." The communication is real but without the normal medium of communication. This is general revelation: information without human words, encounter without verbal address.
4: בְּכָל-הָאָרֶץ יָצָא קַוָּם (bĕkol-hāʾāreṣ yāṣāʾ qawwām, "Yet their proclamation goes out through all the earth") — qaw: a measuring line, a cord; by extension, a line of text or a voice-line. The LXX translates it φθόγγος (sound/voice), which Paul uses in Rom 10:18: "their voice has gone out into all the earth." The silent proclamation nevertheless "goes out" (yāṣāʾ: to go forth, exit) to all the earth. הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ שָׂם אֹהֶל בָּהֶם (hashemesh śām ʾōhel bāhem, "In the heavens he has pitched a tent for the sun") — at v4b the Psalm turns to the sun as the supreme example of the heavens' declaration.
5: וְהוּא כְּחָתָן יֹצֵא מֵחֻפָּתוֹ (wĕhûʾ kĕḥātān yōṣêʾ mêḥuppātô, "which rises like a bridegroom leaving his chamber") — ḥātān: bridegroom; ḥuppāh: the bridal chamber. The sun rising is like a bridegroom emerging from the wedding chamber — full of energy, beauty, and anticipation. יָשִׂישׂ כְּגִבּוֹר לָרוּץ אֹרַח (yāśîś kĕgibbôr lārûṣ ʾōraḥ, "rejoices like a champion eager to run his course") — gibbôr: hero, mighty man; rûṣ: to run. The sun as an eager athlete who cannot wait to begin his course — joy and strength combined.
6: מִקְצֵה הַשָּׁמַיִם מוֹצָאוֹ (miqṣeh hashāmayim môṣāʾô, "Its rising is from one end of the heavens") — the full arc of the sun's path described in spatial terms. וְאֵין נִסְתָּר מֵחַמָּתוֹ (wĕʾên nistār mêḥammātô, "nothing is hidden from its heat") — nistār (niphal participle: hidden, concealed); ḥammāh: heat, warmth. The universal reach of the sun's heat prepares the transition to vv7-9: just as the sun reaches everywhere, so the Torah of the LORD illuminates everything.
7: תּוֹרַת יְהוָה תְּמִימָה מְשִׁיבַת נָפֶשׁ (tôrat YHWH tĕmîmāh mĕshîbat nāpesh, "The instruction of the LORD is perfect, reviving the soul") — the first of six attribute + effect pairs describing the divine word. Tôrāh (instruction, as in Ps 1:2); tĕmîmāh (perfect, unblemished — the sacrificial adjective now applied to Torah: it is the word without flaw). Mĕshîbat nāpesh: "restoring/reviving the soul" — shûb (to return/restore): the instruction brings back what has wandered or declined. The image of the word as nourishment that restores strength.
8: פִּקּוּדֵי יְהוָה יְשָׁרִים (piqqûdê YHWH yĕshārîm, "The precepts of the LORD are right") — piqqûd: what is entrusted for oversight, a commanded charge (plural piqqûdîm). Yāshar (right, straight): the precepts go in a straight line, without deviation. Mĕsamĕḥê lêb: "making the heart rejoice" — the straight path produces the joy of knowing one's feet are rightly placed. מִצְוַת יְהוָה בָּרָה (miṣwat YHWH bārāh, "the commandment of the LORD is radiant") — bārāh: pure, bright, clear; mĕʾîrat ʿênāyim: "enlightening the eyes." The singular miṣwāh (commandment) as the totality of divine command — clear and eye-opening.
9: יִרְאַת יְהוָה טְהוֹרָה (yirʾat YHWH ṭĕhôrāh, "The fear of the LORD is pure") — the sixth term in the series is not a document (tôrāh, ʿêdût, piqqûdîm, miṣwat, mishpĕṭê) but an attitude: the fear of the LORD itself. Ṭāhôr: ritually and morally clean, pure (the priestly purity vocabulary). ʿOmedet lāʿad: "enduring forever" — the fear of the LORD is not a passing emotion but the stable orientation that persists. מִשְׁפְּטֵי-יְהוָה אֱמֶת (mishpĕṭê-YHWH ʾĕmet, "the judgments of the LORD are true") — ʾĕmet (truth, faithfulness, reliability): the judgments correspond to reality.
10: הַנֶּחֱמָדִים מִזָּהָב (hanneḥĕmādîm mizzāhāb, "They are more desirable than gold") — nāḥmad (niphal participle: desired, coveted — from the same root as the tenth commandment's "do not covet"). The divine words are desirable in the same way that gold and honey are desirable. וּמֶתֶק מִדְּבַשׁ וְנֹפֶת צוּפִים (ûmeteq mĕkdbash wĕnōpet ṣûpîm, "sweeter than honey, than honey dripping from the comb") — nōpet: the dripping of a honeycomb, the finest honey; ṣûpîm: flowing, dripping honeycombs. The sweetness is the most refined and abundant form available.
11: גַּם-עַבְדְּךָ נִזְהָר בָּהֶם (gam-ʿabdĕkā nizhar bāhem, "your servant is warned by them") — zāhar (niphal: to be warned, to be enlightened by a warning light — from a root meaning to shine): warned as one is warned by a light that reveals a danger ahead. בְּשָׁמְרָם עֵקֶב רָב (bĕshomrām ʿêqeb rāb, "in keeping them there is great reward") — ʿêqeb: consequence, result, reward (literally "at the heel" — what follows behind). Keeping the word has a heel-trail of great reward.
12: שְׁגִיאוֹת מִי-יָבִין (shĕgîʾôt mî-yābîn, "Who can understand his own errors?") — shĕgîʾāh: an error, a mistake, a wandering from the path (from shāgāh: to wander/err; the same root as shiggāyôn in Ps 7:1). The rhetorical question implies the answer: no one fully understands their own errors. מִנִּסְתָּרוֹת נַקֵּנִי (minnestārôt naqqênî, "Cleanse me from sins I cannot see") — nistarôt: hidden things, secret faults — not morally hidden sins (deliberate concealment) but sins that are invisible to the sinner because of their own moral blind spots. Nāqāh (piel: to cleanse, acquit).
13: גַּם מִזֵּדִים חֲשֹׂךְ עַבְדֶּךָ (gam mizzêdîm ḥăśōk ʿabdekā, "Keep your servant also from willful sins") — zêdîm: the arrogant, presumptuous ones; from zûd (to boil up, to be insolent). The contrast with nistarôt is crucial: v12 covers unconscious errors; v13 covers deliberate, willful transgressions. This exactly maps onto the Num 15 categories of bishgāgāh (by mistake) vs bĕyad rāmāh (with a high/defiant hand) sin — the former has a sacrificial remedy; the latter does not. אָז אֵיתָם (ʾāz ʾêtam, "Then I will be blameless") — "then" (ʾāz) indicates the consequence: kept from willful sin, the Psalmist would attain the blamelessness of Ps 18:23.
14: יִהְיוּ לְרָצוֹן אִמְרֵי-פִי (yihyû lĕrāṣôn ʾimrê-pî, "May the words of my mouth be pleasing in your sight") — rāṣôn: pleasure, delight, acceptance (the same root as rāṣāh used in Ps 18:19 — "he was pleased with me"). The prayer is that speech and meditation (hĕgyôn libbî — the inward hāgāh murmuring of Ps 1:2) be acceptable before God. צוּרִי וְגֹאֲלִי (ṣûrî wĕgōʾălî, "my rock and my redeemer") — the two closing titles: ṣûr (rock, as in Ps 18) and gōʾêl (kinsman-redeemer — the one who has the legal right and relational obligation to buy back what has been lost). The psalm closes with a prayer to the one who is both immovable foundation and closest redeeming kinsman.
